paragraphs.body: The thought that music is good for the soul is not new, but the degree to which it can create positive impacts on humans is something that I’ve often thought about. In the article, “How Music Helps People Heal: The Therapeutic Power of Music”, the author Paul Pellinger explores music from this unconventional angle and describes the physical, emotional, and mental benefits that humans can derive from music. The author presents this idea in several interesting ways, many of which resonated with me. First the author expresses how music can help with stress reduction. Listening to music you enjoy can stimulate the release of stress reducing hormones which can help reduce anxiety, lower blood pressure, and create an overall sense of relaxation. I have experienced this impact first hand, particularly after a stressful day I will turn on my favorite playlists and I can feel my tensions begin to melt away. The author then discusses the power of melody in music and how harmonies and melodies can bring forth specific emotions and provide comfort. I have definitely felt this impact with certain songs that have the ability to instantly transport me back to cherished memories. The author also acknowledges how music can distract from negative thoughts. For instance engaging with music can redirect attention off obsessive and distressing thoughts. I’ve felt this impact profoundly during times of high stress or anxiety, I will use music to try and tune out these thoughts and relax. Overall the author really supports the idea that music has a profound therapeutic power whether you are facing stress, pain, or mental health challenges. Personally, I've always felt a strong connection to music and have recently begun to realize that it is a critical part of my life. A song can change my mood and alter my thought process in ways that other things cannot. Like with any art form however, relationships to music are very personal. The types of music that we listen to, how we listen, and the effects that the music has on us vary in both type and magnitude. I believe that the way that I interact with music may be different than most, but that is ok. Everyone engages with music in their own distinctive way and as Paul Pellinger points out, “Certified music therapists work with individuals of all ages and backgrounds, customizing treatment plans to address specific needs and goals. Sessions may involve listening to music, playing instruments, songwriting, or even group activities.” This illustrates the versatility of music as a therapeutic tool. For many, including myself, music not only serves as therapy but also sparks a deep personal connection.

paragraphs.body: I often notice that I repeatedly play the same songs or artists over and over. When I discover a song or artist I truly enjoy, it becomes the only thing I listen to. I almost become fixated on certain songs because of the feelings they evoke. I thought it was perfectly normal to find a song that connects with me and play it on repeat. However, when I play these songs with my friends, they often ask why I keep choosing the same song and ask me to switch it up. I never noticed a problem with the repetition because it's not just about the fact I like the song, it's about the feeling of comfort and familiarity certain songs bring me. That way of interacting with music is different that the way that my friends interact. These feelings of comfort are so specifically important to me when I'm experiencing anxiety. I have a history of overthinking and I often find myself suffocating in my own thoughts and anxieties making it extremely hard to concentrate. This intentional repetition breaks the cycle of overthinking. Rather than spiraling into anxiety, I’m reminded of happier moments associated with that song. It allows me to focus on these moments and gives my mind a break for the anxious thoughts. Music has such overwhelming power and I hope to always use it to my benefit. It is possible that my personal connection to music and musical preferences will change over time, but I am comforted in knowing that it will always be there as an option to relax, think, celebrate, and connect.

annotations.text: However, when I play these songs with my friends, they often ask why I keep choosing the same song and ask me to switch it up. I never noticed a problem with the repetition because it's not just about the fact I like the song, it's about the feeling of comfort and familiarity certain songs bring me.
annotations.comment: Aha. You're noticing something. Let's figure out what it means in terms of an essay's center of gravity!

paragraphs.body: Now that I am in my first few weeks of college and am away from my home and family I look towards music that can bring me the same comfort and familiarity that my family brings me. For instance, I like to listen to songs that I would normally listen to with my mom or dad or I'll play songs that I really enjoyed during a fun period of my life with the people I love. Some people may think reminding yourself over and over about the things you miss will just make you depressed but that's not how it is for me. Yes, the songs may cause some feelings of sadness but that sadness turns to joy when I'm reminded of the people who are important to me and provide support for me when I'm having anxiety.

annotations.text: Now that I am in my first few weeks of college and am away from my home and family I look towards music that can bring me the same comfort and familiarity that my family brings me.
annotations.comment: Interesting. Does this line point to a potential audience? Who _is_ your audience?

paragraphs.body: In this new chapter of my life, music has become my anchor, a bridge connecting me to home and a reminder of the relationships that support me. It helps me navigate the challenges of adjusting to college life, offering support and familiarity in the middle of this change. When I find myself feeling overwhelmed or homesick, I can turn to these familiar tunes, and they transport me back to moments filled with love and laughter. As I mature and begin to experience more of life, I am comforted to know that I will always have music. People should explore their own personal relationship with music and work to understand its healing powers. It truly is good for the soul but can be even better if you dig in to understand your own personal connection. What types of music do you like to listen to? When do you need music? How do you like to experience that music? Understanding all of this can help you receive all of the benefits that Paul Pellinger describes.
